title: Evolution of the Saddle Point in Antimony Telluride Homologous Superlattices
authors: Yi-Hsin Shen, Shane Smolenski, Ming Wen, Yimo Hou, Eoghan Downey, Jakob Hammond-Renfro, Katharine Moncrieffe, Chun Lin, Makoto Hashimoto, Donghui Lu, Kai Sun, Dominika Zgid, Emanuel Gull, Pierre Ferdinand P. Poudeu, Na Hyun Jo, Rachel S. Goldman
abstract: Combining topological insulators with topological semimetals in the form of homologous superlattices is a promising approach for generating correlated quantum matter based upon Fermi level alignment with band extrema. For antimony telluride, a saddle point is predicted to occur at the M-point, while antimonene layering is predicted to move the M-point valence band towards the Fermi level. To date, the predicted saddle point at the M-point has not yet been demonstrated, and studies of antimony telluride homologous superlattices have been limited to one or two layers of antimonene added to antimony telluride. Here, we present scanning tunneling spectroscopy and angle-resolved photoemission spectroscopy studies of a series of antimony telluride homologous superlattices with two to four layers of antimonene. In addition to demonstrating the presence of a saddle point and associated van Hove singularity near the M-point, we identify the key role of Sb and Te $p_z$ orbital hybridization in driving the van Hove singularity toward the Fermi level.
summary: This paper investigates how adding antimonene layers to antimony telluride superlattices can tune the electronic density of states. By using ARPES and STS, the authors experimentally confirm the existence of a saddle point at the M-point. They demonstrate that increasing the number of layers shifts this saddle point toward the Fermi level due to orbital hybridization. This provides a method to engineer van Hove singularities for studying emergent phenomena like superconductivity or magnetism without external doping.
main_problem: The study aims to experimentally demonstrate and control the alignment of a predicted saddle point and its associated van Hove singularity with the Fermi level in antimony telluride homologous superlattices to access correlated quantum matter.
main_result: The researchers successfully demonstrated the presence of a saddle point at the M-point and showed that increasing the number of antimonene layers monotonically shifts the saddle point energy toward the Fermi level, driven by Sb and Te p_z orbital hybridization.
method: The study combines experimental techniques including ARPES, STS, and XRD with computational scGW (self-consistent GW) calculations and DFT to analyze electronic structure and orbital hybridization.
model_or_system: The physical system consists of antimony telluride (Sb2Te3) homologous superlattices, specifically the (Sb2Te3)1(Sb2)n series where n = 2, 3, and 4, which transition from a topological semiconductor to a semimetal.
key_observables: Band gap size, saddle point energy (E_SP), van Hove singularities (vHs), effective masses, Fermi surface topology, and orbital-resolved density of states.
important_parameters: Number of antimonene layers (n = 2, 3, 4), M-point energy, and Sb/Te p_z orbital hybridization strength.
main_assumptions: The study assumes consistent surface termination (specifically QL termination) for comparable ARPES measurements across the superlattice series.
figures_summary: Figure 1 shows structural models and XRD patterns; Figure 2 presents STS differential conductance spectra; Figure 4 illustrates scGW-computed band dispersion and electron density; Figure 5 shows ARPES-measured energy band dispersions and STS vHs identification.
paper_structure: The paper begins with structural characterization via XRD, moves to electronic structure characterization using STS and ARPES to identify band gap closing and the saddle point, provides computational verification of the orbital hybridization mechanism via scGW, and concludes with a discussion on the systematic tuning of the van Hove singularity.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1. (a) Models for antimony telluride homologous superlattices, with Sb and Te atoms represented as red and green circles. The repeat units, illustrated with colored boxes, consists of antimony telluride (Sb2Te3), with or without two to four layers of antimonene (Sb2). Sb2Te3 (red) and homologous superlattices containing three (green) and four (orange) layers of antimonene belong to the rhombohedral R-3M space group with ABC stacking, while antimony telluride homologous superlattices with two layers of antimonene (blue) crystallize in the trigonal P-3M1 space group. (b) Powder X-ray diffraction (XRD) patterns for Sb2Te3 (red) and homologous superlattices containing two (blue), three...

FIG. 2. (a) Plots of differential conductance vs. sample bias voltage, collected at 77K, for antimony telluride homologous superlattices with 2, 3, and 4 antimonene layers, i.e. (Sb2Te3)1 (Sb2)n (n = 2, 3, 4), in comparison with that of antimony telluride, Sb2Te3. The sample voltages correspond to the energy relative to the Fermi level, and all spectra are vertically offset for clarity. For Sb2Te3, well-defined band-edges, with a band gap of ≈0.22 eV, is apparent, consistent with earlier reports [11, 35]. In contrast, for the antimony telluride homologous superlattices, the band gap closes [13] and additional states emerge above the Fermi level, as indicated by downward pointing arrows. (b)...

FIG. 3. Electronic structure of antimony telluride homologous superlattices. (a) Surface and bulk Brillouin zones (BZ), including the trigonal surface BZ for both antimony telluride and its homologous superlattices (top), the hexagonal bulk BZ for antimony telluride homologous superlattices containing two layers of antimonene (middle), and the rhomobohedral bulk BZ for antimony telluride homologous superlattices with three and four layers of antimonene (bottom).(b) Computed electron energy band dispersion along high symmetry paths for antimony telluride homologous superlattices containing two layers of antimonene, (Sb2Te3)1(Sb2)2. (c) Computed density of states as a function of energy for...

FIG. 4. The band dispersion and electron density for (Sb2Te3)1(Sb2)2 computed by scGW near the saddle point in the vicinity of M. (Top) Nearly flat band dispersion along the Γ-M-K path in the vicinity of the saddle point. Grey points correspond to maxima in the DOS, with red dots mark- ing points within ± 0.23 ˚A-1 of the M-point. (Bottom) Real space depiction of the electron density at M-, M, and M+, constructed by summing all symmetrized atomic orbital con- tributions, each weighted by its spectral intensity fraction at the corresponding DOS peak. FIG. 5. Evolution of saddle point in antimony telluride homologous superlattices containing 2, 3, and 4 antimonene layers, i.e. (Sb2Te3)1(Sb2)n (n = 2, 3, 4): (a)-(c) ARPES-measured electron energy dispersions along the Γ - M - K path in the vicinity of M for antimonene telluride homologous superlattices with (a) 2, (b) 3, and (c) 4 antimonene layers.
